data.title: National Health Investors: A Healthcare REIT Ready For The Senior Housing Supercycle
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

National Health Investors (NHI) is up quite a bit over the past year, as the company is actively improving, recovering and transitioning to a SHOP-oriented model, although I believe it still offers an opportunity to get a solid dividend and exposure to a very significant long-term trend in the senior housing industry.

As we can see, NHI reported an even better Normalized FAD of ~$62.25 million in Q3'25 (vs. $49.38 million in Q3'24), meaning about $1.31 per share compared to only $1.13 in Q3'24. That's a very significant increase despite the higher number of shares, as a result of a mix of factors, including collecting $5.1 million in repayments on previously deferred rent, SHOP transition (SHOP NOI up 63% YoY) and slightly lower interest expenses.

Still, for the full year, NHI improved their guidance quite a bit, expecting Normalized FAD of $231.7 million to $233.5 million compared to the previous guidance of $227.9 million to $229.8 million. At midpoint, that's roughly $232.6 million, placing them at a P/FAD ratio of ~16 based on the current ~$3.73 billion market cap, which is not bad given the overall business quality and growth potential in this market.

Financially, based on NHI's latest report, we see a good position for a REIT, with a relatively low amount of leverage, a decent cash position and a lot of assets to cover everything. Once again, the PB may seem low when the equity is at ~$1.49 billion compared to the ~$3.73 billion market cap, but we should keep in mind that these assets are not only depreciated for tax reasons, but their value is displayed at the price of acquisition, not adjusted for their real market value - which can be much higher even in today's market.

What's even more attractive is that NHI has a very impressive debt maturity schedule, with barely any debt due over the next half a decade, as most of it is due after 2029 - plus significant liquidity still available under their line of credit that should allow them to enjoy potential opportunities.

Dividends

After a few years of struggle due to the pandemic's obvious effects on this type of company, NHI is back to slowly growing its dividend, currently paying a yield of around 4.7%, representing roughly $175 million per year, for a payout of roughly 75.37% based on the midpoint FAD they estimate, which is very solid and definitely has plenty of room for upside if they want to even if they don't achieve that much growth.

Risks & Opportunities

REITs (especially healthcare ones) have been affected significantly in the years since the pandemic, as not only did the occupancy rates take a significant hit while costs rose and tenants had issues, but the cost of external capital also increased significantly as a result of the rate hikes.

This is ultimately normal, as rate hikes lead to higher treasury rates and SOFR, consequently causing higher costs of debt, while the market now has a much more attractive alternative if it wants a 4% to 5% return (the theoretically risk free treasuries), which also pushes the yield of REITs up as stock prices fall to adjust to the new risk-reward profile.

As a result, REITs, which have to pay most of what they make back to us through dividends, are suddenly pushed into an environment with higher debt costs, higher costs of issuing stock (the dividend is basically the yield they have to pay, similar to debt) as well as internal issues caused by tenants being late to pay rent or even filing for bankruptcies.

This is completely normal for the entire asset class, and I wanted to reiterate this because more and more people are calling REITs bad investments because of their performance in the past 2-5 years, which is a shame because I also believe these have been some of the best years to accumulate them.

As a side note, although I don't own NHI (for now), I do own Omega (OHI) with an average cost of $28.7 per share, meaning I am not only up about 54% since 2022 when I started buying, but I also locked in a sustainable yield of cost of about 9.33% - and that's without taking into account any potential increases. That's more than I could've asked from an asset like this, especially since it also offers excellent exposure to long-term trends, which I'll mention below.

However, although rate cuts can help, we should keep in mind that they aren't the only thing that dictates long-term rates, as the market factors in much more than just that, including the prospects of the country, its growth expectations, inflation, etc., which is where the issue might be.

Right now, there are several other factors that the market is worrying about even beyond the country's growth expectations, with the December Fed minutes showing a 9-3 vote in favor of the cut, which is the biggest disagreement since 2019, while some participants indicated that they believe a pause is the right call following this cut.

Meanwhile, we have rising fear around the Fed's independence, as Powell's term ends in May 2026 and Trump will likely nominate a person that shares his views this time. Although that's still just 1 vote out of 12 in FOMC decisions, we're still talking about a very influential person. Even if the market would not listen to what this person says, we would still lose important insights into the near-term that can lead to more volatility and rising uncertainty.

Especially following the DOJ criminal investigation into Powell, rising uncertainty around Greenland, Iran, Venezuela, tariffs, and other macro worries, we can see the market demanding better yields for the new risks it perceives, which would be directly reflected in the cost of capital of these companies, including NHI.

However, over the long-term - which should be the main focus when investing in a company like this - the trend is quite clearly in NHI's favor, in my opinion. As OHI also mentioned, we're talking about a very fragmented market, meaning that they should have plenty of room for acquisitions, with their status as a public company allowing them to issue stock and tap into external capital that's not available for a private company, allowing them to enjoy the massive potential boom in demand from Baby Boomers. As NHI's CEO also mentioned during their Q3 Earnings Call:

The momentum at NHI is building. We are well positioned and laser-focused to capitalize on the generational growth in the senior housing industry over the next decade. As I noted last quarter, we have methodically invested in creating a strong foundation across all of our disciplines that will allow us to significantly expand our presence in private pay senior housing, where we see the greatest risk-adjusted returns.

Meanwhile, the transition to SHOP and basically cutting tenants as they focus on managing these properties themselves also limits the exposure to these companies (if they play it right, they can only keep the healthier tenants) while improving margins, although it also increases the operational risks.

The very important point is that there is plenty of room for occupancy to grow from the current ~87.5%, even though it improved over the past couple of years, and self-management can allow them to fully enjoy a potential growth in occupancy as demand rises, which wouldn't really make a meaningful difference if they were just simply renting these properties.

In other words, the rent is basically the same for their properties no matter if the occupancy is 75% or 100%, while managing these themselves basically allows them to get exposure to a potential increase or decrease.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a diverse set of peers such as Omega Healthcare Investors, CareTrust REIT (CTRE), Welltower (WELL), Ventas (VTR), Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA) and LTC Properties (LTC), we see an overall average valuation, although most of these peers outperformed NHI in recent years, although it's worth noting that NHI has one of lowest levels of leverage and is in a good financial position.

Risks Reiterated

Despite the company's financial health and potential for more internal improvements, we should remember that the focus on SHOP can also increase their operational risks, exposing them even more to the environment they play in.

Meanwhile, even though the long-term should be in favor of this industry, the environment can still get even worse before it improves, while elevated external capital costs can limit the company's expansion potential, especially as they are directly impacted by macro factors even more as they are switching to SHOP.

Valuation

Starting with NHI's guidance of normalized FAD of $232.6 million in 2025, let's assume a 4.5% CAGR over the first 5 years (similar to consensus FFO estimates) thanks to their switch to SHOP, potential occupancy/margin improvements and other expansion activities, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a 9% discount rate and 47.623 million shares outstanding as of their Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $4.13 billion, for an intrinsic value of $86.69, which is still above the current ~$79.23.

Conclusion

I rate NHI a Buy thanks to their healthy financials, very manageable debt schedule and attractive dividend, as well as significant long-term potential thanks to macro tailwinds and SHOP focus. The overall risk-reward seems to be in the investment's favor, as even though they are exposed to industry-specific risks, the long-term tailwinds and solid dividend could make this quite rewarding, especially for a long-term dividend/pension-oriented portfolio.

data.title: Buy The Dip: Quality Dividend Growth Stocks Getting Way Too Cheap
data.description:
Right now, the market is offering up some very attractive opportunities to buy quality dividend growth stocks (SCHD) at deep discounts due to negative headlines, even though the underlying fundamentals remain sound. Today’s article will highlight two of these names in particular. While they are very different businesses from each other and are selling off due to different headlines, they share three things in common: strong cash flow visibility, conservatively structured balance sheets, and substantial valuation dislocations that make them very hard to ignore.

Invitation Homes and the Policy Fear Driving Its Sell-off

The first opportunity to talk about is Invitation Homes (INVH), which has sold off sharply due to headwinds from rising interest rates (which has slowed their ability to acquire properties at attractive spreads) and has fallen another leg lower recently on fears that the Trump administration’s push to ban large institutions from buying single-family homes could effectively break their growth model, which relies on acquiring new homes to grow the portfolio and ultimately cash flow per share.

INVH is the largest publicly traded single-family rental REIT (VNQ) in the United States, as it owns about 86,000 homes and manages 16,000 additional single-family properties. It primarily focuses its homeownership on infill, high-growth Sunbelt, and coastal markets. This boosts it due to strong in-migration in these regions.

Additionally, the business benefits from a structural housing undersupply and a market dynamic in which homeownership has never been more expensive due to the combination of high housing prices and high mortgage rates. Thus, renting is hundreds of dollars per month cheaper than owning in INVH’s main markets.

As a result, it is enjoying solid organic growth with a 2.1% blended rental rate growth, a 4.3% renewal rent growth rate, and a high occupancy rate of about 95.9%. Additionally, there is a build-to-rent delivery decline, which is further boosting supply-demand dynamics in INVH’s favor.

INVH also generates very stable cash flows thanks to its well-diversified portfolio of essential, affordable housing real estate, which has fairly inelastic demand, given that everyone needs housing, and renting a home is so much cheaper than buying a home right now.

Additionally, the company’s balance sheet is fairly conservatively positioned with a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.2 times, which is below management’s long-term target range of 5.5 times to 6.0 times. This gives them additional flexibility to be able to invest opportunistically, either in stock buybacks or acquisitions, as well as be able to support the dividend even if the business were to encounter a cash flow dip.

Additionally, even if, in a worst-case scenario, they were banned from buying new homes, they could use their balance sheet flexibility to take advantage of their very cheap valuation, which currently stands at around a 30% discount to net asset value, to buy back stock aggressively and create a significant amount of NAV per share accretion for shareholders. Not only that, but they could pursue a partial or full liquidation of their portfolio as well, which would be very value accretive relative to their current stock price.

The fact that their retained cash flow right now is between 2.5% and 3% of market cap for a full year means they could buy back a decent chunk of stock if they did not have any other place to reinvest that capital. Meanwhile, it will also pay out a pretty solid 4.5% dividend yield, which is its highest yield since going public in 2017 and nearly twice its historical average dividend yield of about 2.5%. Additionally, it is priced at an FFO multiple of about 15.8 times, which is very cheap compared to its historical average of 23.8 times.

When you combine the quality of the underlying performance, the strong balance sheet, and the attractive long-term fundamentals in its markets, as well as its very cheap valuation and attractive dividend yield, INVH presents very attractive risk-adjusted return potential for shareholders. While the near-term story may be suppressed due to negative headlines, over the long term, the resilient intrinsic value of its holdings, the attractive dividend yield, the stable cash flow profile, and its deep discount to net asset value mean it should deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns.

Hess Midstream’s Fee-based Model and Natural Gas Growth Tilt

The next opportunity I am going to discuss is Hess Midstream (HESM), which is a midstream (AMLP) company that, while traditionally structured as an MLP, issues a 1099 tax form. HESM has pulled back sharply over concerns that its future earnings power could be impaired if CVX reduces operations in the Bakken, where HESM is concentrated.

This concern was heightened when HESM recently reduced its oil operations in the Bakken from previous expectations, leading to HESM pulling back slightly on its guidance.

In July of last year, HESM was guiding for "greater than $1.25B of financial flexibility expected through 2027 for potential incremental share repurchases." However, today, that guidance has been tempered to "~$1.0B of Adjusted Free Cash Flow after Distributions through 2028 for incremental shareholder returns and debt repayment." That means that, in addition to a reduction of $250M in the number, the focus has shifted from purely being on share repurchases to both shareholder returns and debt repayment. This signals a more cautious approach to capital allocation, which also seems to convey a growing uncertainty over the future production profile feeding HESM's infrastructure.

That being said, in our view, the sell-off is overblown. HESM is a fee-based Bakken-focused midstream operator that has 90% of its revenues protected by minimum volume commitments with long-term contracts that extend through 2033 in some cases. Additionally, its cash flows are 100% fee-based, so it has no direct commodity price exposure, and its minimum volume commitments are set at 80% of its three-year forward nominations.

Not only that, but gas represents 75% of its revenues, which gives it a growth tilt, since natural gas demand is growing, especially relative to crude. Not only that, but it also has a water gathering business that provides additional diversification alongside its crude and gas businesses. The company is also generating solid growth with expected 10% year-over-year EBITDA growth based on its full-year guidance, and its Q3 adjusted EBITDA improved sequentially from Q2.

Moreover, its CapEx is expected to fall substantially moving forward as it removed the CapEx gas plant from its forward plans, and Chevron (CVX) has announced less crude oil drilling activity in the Bakken, which means fewer operations on that front and an increasing tilt toward natural gas. This means that it should be able to generate substantial free cash flow in the near future, which it plans to use to continue growing its distribution at at least a 5% annual rate while also buying back stock aggressively. In Q3 alone, it bought back $100 million worth of stock and increased its distribution 2.4% sequentially.

Meanwhile, its balance sheet is in great shape with leverage at just ~3x. On a next 12-month basis, its dividend yield is very attractive at 9% and is very well covered by free cash flow. Not only that, but it trades at one of the lowest EV-to-EBITDA multiples in the midstream sector and also has among the very best free cash flow yields.

This presents a disconnect because the market is pricing it as if it is a rapidly declining business, and yet it is actually growing with a growth tilt toward natural gas. Yes, there is some risk given its concentration in the Bakken and the fact that it is heavily dependent on CVX, but all signs point to continued growth on the net when factoring in its natural gas assets, while its oil assets appear to be stable for the near future. Even if its oil assets do decline in the outer years, continued growth in natural gas should help to offset this.

Moreover, it has a huge margin of safety built into its valuation, with a strong balance sheet, aggressive buybacks, and dividend growth, thereby offsetting much of its concentration risk and potential weakened growth outlook beyond the next three to five years.

The biggest thing to monitor moving forward with HESM is what the minimum volume commitment framework will look like after 2028, including the potential for declined activity by CVX in the Bakken. However, its continued investments in growing its natural gas production in the region bode well for at least HESM’s natural gas business continuing to thrive in the years to come.

Investor Takeaway

While both of these businesses have fallen recently due to headline risk, namely CVX’s reduced production outlook for oil in the Bakken and INVH slumping in the wake of Trump’s potential appeal on house purchases by major institutions, both companies remain fundamentally sound and growing, with attractive, well-covered dividends, a path toward highly accretive buybacks, solid balance sheets, and ultimately large margins of safety to deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns for patient investors. These are the sorts of opportunities I pounce on in my 7% to 8% yielding, low-beta, and outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Quantum Corporation: High-Risk Turnaround Play - Speculative Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Quantum Corporation, or "Quantum" (NASDAQ:QMCO), previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Twelve months ago, I initiated coverage of beleaguered data storage solutions provider Quantum Corporation with a "Sell" rating after the stock was inadvertently picked up by speculative investors and traders as a perceived quantum computing play.

This turned out to be a great call, as the stock price has cratered by almost 90% over the past year:

However, it might be time to change my assessment of the company after Quantum announced better-than-expected preliminary Q3/FY2026 results on Thursday with sales coming in above the high end of the guidance range provided by management in the Q2 earnings release.

(...) Based on unaudited financials, the Company expects the following:

  • Revenue of approximately $72.7 million, above the high-end of the guided range of $67 million, plus or minus $2 million
  • GAAP gross margin of approximately 38%
  • GAAP operating expenses of approximately $28.1 million
  • Non-GAAP adjusted operating expenses of approximately $26.9 million, within the provided guidance range

Quantum expects to report its full results for the fiscal third quarter of 2026 by mid-February 2026.

Over the past year, the company has been busy dealing with accounting issues that required the restatement of past financial results.

Following several quarters of operational underperformance, Quantum also decided to replace senior management.

That said, sales execution started to improve in Q2/FY2026 with backlog increasing to approximately $25 million compared to the company's historical target range of $8 million to $10 million with strong momentum across all product lines as outlined by management on the conference call in November.

Based on Quantum's preliminary results, I would expect Adjusted EBITDA to be in a range of $3 million to $4 million, well above management's guidance of "positive $1 million, plus or minus $1 million".

Last month, Quantum shareholders approved a debt restructuring that resulted in the exchange of $54.7 million in term debt held by Dialectic Technology SPV LLC ("Dialectic") into new convertible notes despite less-than-stellar terms (emphasis added by author):

The Convertible Notes will mature on the third anniversary of the closing and will have an interest rate of 10% per annum, payable in kind, compounded annually.

The initial conversion price of the Convertible Notes will be $10.00 per share of common stock (Conversion Price), subject to adjustment for stock splits and similar events.

The Conversion Price is also subject to adjustment on the last day of each calendar quarter for three consecutive quarters following closing, but in any event the Conversion Price will not be reset below $4.00 per share of common stock.

Following the six-month anniversary of closing, if certain conditions are met, the Company may elect to require the conversion of a portion of the total outstanding amount of any Convertible Notes into shares of common stock at the then current Conversion Price.

Effective after the close of business on December 31, the conversion price was adjusted to $7.805 based on the 30-day value-weighted average trading price ("VWAP") of the company's common share preceding the reset date.

Following the price adjustment, Dialectic would receive approximately 7.0 million new common shares upon conversion.

Please note that Dialectic also extracted dilution-protected "Forbearance Warrants" to purchase 2.65 million new common shares at an initial exercise price of $8.81.

The exercise price and the number of shares underlying the Forbearance Warrant are subject to adjustment in the event of specified events, including dilutive issuances at a price lower than the exercise price of the Forbearance Warrant, a subdivision or combination of the common stock, a reclassification of the common stock or specified dividend payments, subject to certain limitations as set forth in the Forbearance Warrant. Upon exercise, the aggregate exercise price may be paid, at the Selling Stockholder's election, in cash or on a net issuance basis, based upon the then current market price of the common stock at the time of exercise.

In layman's terms, share issuances to Dialectic upon note conversions would lower the exercise price of the warrants.

Assuming full conversion and exercise of all warrants, Dialectic's stake in the company would amount to 41.3% based on 13.72 million outstanding common shares as of November 11, 2025.

Given the terms of the convertible notes and forbearance warrants, Dialectic would be incentivized to short Quantum's common stock in order to maximize gains. That said, short interest has actually declined in recent months:

For valuation purposes, I will stick with the current conversion price of $7.805, which, in combination with assumed warrant exercises, would result in outstanding shares increasing to 23.385 million. Please note that under this scenario, Quantum would receive an estimated $20.7 million in cash proceeds.

However, it is more likely that Dialectic would exercise the warrants on a net issuance basis, which means that Quantum would issue a lower amount of shares equal to the intrinsic value of the warrant but not receive any cash proceeds.

In addition, my valuation exercise does not assume additional share issuances under the company's Standby Equity Purchase Agreement ("SEPA") with a division of Yorkville Advisors Global.

Moreover, my estimates assume continued operational improvement in FY2027 and FY2028, with headwinds from skyrocketing memory prices being more than offset by increasing demand for the company's cold data storage solutions.

Should Quantum's turnaround take hold, I would expect market participants to value the company at an EV/Adjusted EBITDA multiple in a range of 10x to 15x, which is still significantly below much larger competitors from the likes of Pure Storage (PSTG), Seagate Technology (STX), and Western Digital Corporation (WDC).

At 12x estimated FY2028 EV/Adjusted EBITDA, the price target would calculate to $12.50. Keep in mind that my valuation is based on rather optimistic assumptions regarding potential dilution and the company's ability to execute on its turnaround efforts.

However, it is important to note that analysts currently expect Q4 revenues to decline by approximately 15% on a quarter-over-quarter basis, which I consider way too pessimistic despite some anticipated seasonality.

Should management's Q4 guidance exceed moderate consensus expectations significantly and backlog remain strong, more speculative investors and traders could be attracted to the stock, particularly with the company's name suggesting a focus on the still red-hot quantum computing space. However, while Quantum recently announced a quantum computing collaboration, its primary focus remains on data storage solutions.

Please note also that a rising share price would help keep convertible note dilution in check.

In sum, only the most speculative investors should consider an investment in this early stage of a potential turnabout and ahead of management's initial fourth quarter guidance next month.

Will certainly a high-risk investment, I am upgrading Quantum Corporation's shares from "Sell" to "Speculative Buy" with a price target of $12.50.

Risks

As outlined above already, an investment in the shares at this early stage of a potential turnaround should be considered highly speculative, particularly given uncertainties regarding the impact of skyrocketing memory prices on the company's business. Should management warn of margin pressures and lower demand for the company's flash storage products without an offsetting increase in sales for the company's cold data storage solutions, the stock price would likely drop to new 52-week lows.

Bottom Line

Following many years of sales declines and resulting cash outflows, Quantum Corporation's business has been showing signs of life in recent months.

On Thursday, the company announced better-than-expected preliminary Q3/FY2026 results, which should translate into meaningfully positive Adjusted EBITDA.

To continue its turnaround, Quantum will have to execute flawlessly in order to balance risks from skyrocketing memory prices impacting its more advanced flash storage products with a potential resurgence in demand for the company's cold data storage offerings.

In addition, the overhang from convertible notes and warrants recently issued to Dialectic is likely to persist in the near- to medium term.

Moreover, there might be additional dilution from sales under the above-discussed SEPA agreement.

On the flip side, consensus expectations for the final quarter of fiscal year 2026 appear to be on the low side, thus increasing the chance of management providing upside guidance next month.

With so many moving parts, the company remains a high-risk investment only suited for highly speculative investors and traders, particularly at this early stage of a potential turnaround. Put should new management indeed return the business to sustained growth and improved margins, upside in the shares could be north of 50% and potentially even higher.

As a result, I am upgrading Quantum Corporation's shares from "Sell" to "Speculative Buy" with a price target of $12.50.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Power Producers Spark Higher
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump to push for emergency auction to get tech firms funding new power plants. (00:27) Disney's (DIS) Kennedy hands off 'Star Wars' leadership. (02:03) Dimon says ‘no chance’ on Fed chair job. (03:00)

This is an abridged transcript.

Power producers such as Constellation Energy (CEG) and Vistra (VST) and NRG Energy (NRG) are on our biggest movers list this morning.

According to multiple media reports, The White House is expected to issue a directive today to propose an emergency power auction in which tech companies would bid for new plants built.

According to WSJ, citing people familiar with the matter, under the directive, power grid operator PJM Interconnection will be told to hold a power auction for tech companies to bid on 15-year contracts to build new power plants.

PJM is the largest grid operator in the U.S., spanning 13 states. Data centers built amid the AI boom have strained grid capacity and raised costs in recent power auctions.

The White House said its National Energy Dominance Council and the governors of several states, including Pennsylvania, Ohio, and Virginia, want to try to push the grid operator to take urgent steps to boost energy supply and curb price hikes.

CEG is up 1.1% after rising 3.2% on Thursday. Vistra is up 2% after seeing a 6.6% increase and NRG is up 1.8% after closing 5.8% higher.

It's time for a new wave to pick up the lightsaber for Disney (DIS), as Kathleen Kennedy is stepping down from her longtime position as the sensei of Star Wars.

Kennedy is departing Lucasfilm, where she's held a leadership position for nearly 14 years. She joined the production company as co-chair with Star Wars creator/director George Lucas, and became its only leader shortly afterward when Disney (DIS) bought the company and Lucas exited.

Dave Filoni, chief creative officer and a Lucas protégé, will take over creative leadership of Lucasfilm, while President/General Manager Lynwen Brennan will act as co-president and lead the business side. They'll report to Disney Entertainment Co-Chairman Alan Bergman.

Kennedy plans to return to full-time producing, including finishing upcoming Lucasfilm projects The Mandalorian and Grogu and Star Wars: Starfighter.

Jamie Dimon, chief executive officer of JPMorgan Chase (JPM), indicated he has no interest in becoming chair of the Federal Reserve.

Asked whether he would consider the role, Dimon replied there was “absolutely, positively no chance—no way, no how, for any reason.”

He was more open, however, to the idea of leading the U.S. Treasury. "The president calls you up and asks you to do something – you should consider it, so I would take the call and consider it," he said. "I like my job, and I’ve been my own boss for pretty much 25 years."

Dimon also said he wants to remain CEO of JPMorgan Chase (JPM) for at least another five years.

Dimon, who is 69 years old, has led the bank since 2006 and is one of the longest-serving CEOs on Wall Street.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

France warns U.S. against Greenland seizure as Europe sends troops to island

U.S. megabanks reduce headcount by most in nearly a decade - report

Tech earnings should be AI validation sign: Wedbush

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 1% at $59/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.3% at $95,000. Gold is down 0.2% at $4,607.

The FTSE 100 is little changed and the DAX is up 0.2%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT) -4.9% Shares slipped after mixed results. The logistics company earned a better-than-expected profit despite a 2% drop in revenue.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 9:15 am Industrial Production

  • 10:00 am Housing Market Index

Another item to add to your calendar but it’s not for today. It’s our Investing Experts Live-Top Ideas for 2026 event. Investing Experts is the sister podcast to Wall Street Breakfast. But this is a live event. It starts at noon on January 27th. You’ll hear from experts about where growth and income opportunities are emerging this year. They’ll discuss fundamentals and navigating market volatility with confidence. I’ll leave a link to register for the free event in the show notes section.

data.title: Fallen Angels Deliver Again With An Eye Towards 2026
data.description:
Fallen angels outperformed broad high yield in 2025, driven by security selection, longer duration, and resilient credit quality. Attractive yields and selective downgrades support 2026.

Fallen angels outperformed the broad high yield market by 0.53% in 2025 (9.03% vs. 8.50%), despite underperforming in Q4 by 0.25% (1.11% vs 1.35%). In a year marked by macro volatility and shifting rate expectations, fallen angels once again demonstrated their ability to deliver resilient returns through a volatile macro backdrop.

While fallen angels lagged in parts of Q4 amid renewed rate volatility, those short-term headwinds were more than offset by consistent issuer level outperformance earlier in the year. The outperformance in 2025 was driven primarily by security selection within sectors. In particular, some of the newer fallen angels (Basic Industry: Celanese and Huntsman, Autos: Nissan and Aptiv and Consumer Goods: Whirlpool) were top contributors to outperformance vs broad high yield. As has historically been the case, many of these newer fallen angels entered the high yield market at discounted prices following forced selling, creating attractive entry points ahead of a technical recovery. Duration also contributed positively to outperformance as fallen angels were on average 1.7 years longer than high yield. 2025 marks 15 years of outperformance out of the last 22 calendar years for fallen angels’ over broad high yield.

2025 Total Returns

Source: ICE Data Services, VanEck.

2025 in Review

  • Fallen angels outpaced rising stars in 2025 for the first time since 2020. Fallen angel activity reflected idiosyncratic credit migration rather than broad-based sector downgrades. Fallen angels added more than $22bn to the index market value while rising stars removed approximately $5bn, which contrasts with the pattern seen in the prior years.
  • The Basic Industry sector was most impacted by fallen angels, as Celanese, Huntsman International and FMC Corp added approximately $10bn. No rising star removed more than $1bn of face value. The fallen angel index finished 2025 with Basic Industry, Retail, Telecom and Autos as the sectors with more than 10% each of exposure. These will be the sectors to watch in 2026 as they, combined, represented more than 50% of the fallen angel index.
  • Investors closely monitored President Trump’s feed, tariff announcements, and inflation and labor market indicators. Towards the middle to end of the year, Fed rate cuts expectations took center stage. This resulted in continued yield curve steepening (2-10s), which began in 2023, from 32bps to 71bps. The 2Y yield decreased the most to 3.47% while the 30Y yield increased slightly to 4.84%.
  • Corporate balance sheets remained strong in 2025. Fallen angels had no defaults while the broad high yield market had 7 issuers for a total number of 14 bonds, adding to $14.2bn of face value. The absence of defaults among fallen angels in 2025 highlights the higher-quality bias of the segment, particularly when compared with pockets of stress that persisted in the lower-rated tiers of high yield.
  • Credit spreads were volatile in the beginning of the year but ended very close to where they started and below long-term historical averages. IG spreads tightened by 3bps to 79bps, HY tightened by 11bps to 281and fallen angels tightened by 4bps to 245.

2026 Expectations

  • We believe that long-term rates are unlikely to significantly decrease but short-term rates may decline modestly as monetary policy becomes friendlier. Interest rates are closer to neutral with the median dot plot only showing one 25bps rate cut in 2026.
  • Fallen angels’ yield of 6.36%, though lower than at the beginning of 2025, is still above the 5y and 10y averages, offers higher quality compared to the past 10 years. Spreads continue to be tight but the higher quality of fallen angel’s vs broad high yield, may play in our favor if spreads were to widen. Spreads are still a very low percentage of overall yield (currently at 39%), so movements in Treasury yields will likely drive price returns.
  • Balance sheets remain strong, which give credit markets the ability to absorb slower growth, as many are expecting, in 2026 without demanding wider spreads. While absolute spreads remain tight, fallen angels enter 2026 with a healthy credit profile, suggesting less downside asymmetry should growth slow or volatility resurface.
  • In terms of rating migrations, 2026 is expected to remain idiosyncratic in nature as there is no specific sector on watch for widespread downgrades. As it occurs with downgrades, fallen angels are offering distinct sector exposures vs the high yield market.
  • JP Morgan expects approximately $84bn of fallen angels, though the majority of this number is tied back to potential downgrades of Ford and Paramount. Importantly, much of the projected downgrade volume remains concentrated in a small number of large issuers, limiting the likelihood of broad-based pressure across the high yield market.
  • Consistent with broader high yield forecasts, we expect returns in 2026 to be driven more by carry and rate movements than by spread compression, but with downside risks that may arise from unexpected labor market weakness, another flare up in broader credit concerns or a host of political or geopolitical factors. Given how tight spreads are, we see little upside from further spread tightening from here.

As we start 2026, we believe fallen angels have the potential to perform well in the context of a lower-growth, rate-sensitive environment, supported by higher quality and attractive yields with a history of outperformance.

Fallen Angels Overall Statistics: Credit spreads widened early to mid-October as trade tensions resurfaced, the US government was shut down but tightened back at the end as the Fed delivered a cut. November was somewhat similar as spreads widened early in the month but then trended back down to finish the year very close to where they started it. Volatility elevated, particularly in first half of the year. Liberation Day in April, the second round of tariffs in August, and the Government shutdown all contributed to spread volatility this year. Ultimately, fallen angel spreads ended just 4bps tighter while broad high yield spreads ended 11bps tighter than at the beginning of the year. Yields followed the spreads path outlined above, as they reach year highs in early April (7.69 for fallen angels and 8.66 for broad high yield) but then trended back down to finish the in 6-handle. Both finished the year close to their multi-year lows, especially broad high yield as it is reaching levels not seen since early 2022. Fallen angels’ duration was flat during the quarter, while broad high yield continued to hit all-time lows (2.80) during the year as issuers deferred refinancing. Duration was a major contributor to outperformance this past year, as fallen angels were longer, on average, 1.7 years longer than high yield.

data.title: Lockheed Martin: Why The Golden Dome Project Is Now Critical For The Stock
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) shares have surged 17.9% since my last report outperforming the S&P 500’s 4% gain. In my report published in October, I marked the stock a buy as I believe that the rather boring earnings that Lockheed Martin put out provided an attractive entry point. In this report, I discuss the company’s share price development, discuss the earnings expectations and update my price target.

December Marked The Bottom For Lockheed Martin

In my prior earnings discussion, I called the rather boring earnings a perfect moment for a buy. While the stock did return to nearly 18% and outperformed the market, I also have to be reasonable and admit that the actual “perfect moment” to buy Lockheed Martin stock was in December. That has been the case for many names in the aerospace and defense industry as we saw tension in the Caribbean mounting. The real outperformance against the market came after the capture of Maduro and President Trump floating the idea of a $1.5 trillion defense budget.

A higher defense budget would definitely be a positive for prime defense contractors such as Lockheed Martin as it could create a more realistic budget path for the Golden Dome project, which may be one of Lockheed’s main growth drivers after it saw Boeing winning the contract for the US Air Force version of the next generation fighter jet while it was dropped from the selection list for the US Navy version leaving Northrop Grumman in a good position to win the contract. At the same time, we do note that a $1.5 trillion budget would be a significant increase from the current $900 billion budget. While that budget may be fitting for the needs to develop capabilities in the areas of hypersonics, missile defense, cyber, space, drones and counter-drone solutions an increase to the budget would certainly increase the government debt and therefore can count on resistance. So, a higher defense budget is definitely not a certainty and with a bit of luck a more balanced defense budget would be around $1 trillion.

For the fourth quarter, analysts are expecting Lockheed Martin to grow 6.6% to $19.85 billion with estimates ranging from $19.4 billion to $20.15 billion. Over the past six months, we note that estimates have increased 0.9%. So, there is no major shift in expected revenues. Growth in the near-term is likely driven by higher missile and missile defense sales. Over the past three months revenue estimates increase with 12 upward revisions and 2 revisions down. However, we note that this only produced a 0.1% lift to the consensus. Analysts are expecting earnings per share of $6.21 indicating a 19.4% decrease.

Lockheed Martin Is Not The Most Attractive Aerospace And Defense Stock

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Lockheed Martin, I had a $584.24 price target. As we are in 2026 and I tend to base my price target on the upcoming year’s earnings, the price target would be $629.83 indicating 9% upside. That is not a lot of upside and if it weren’t for the company to trade at substantial discount, there would also be no buy rating for Lockheed Martin.

We note that EBITDA estimates for 2025-2027 came down by less than a percent and the CAGR measured from 2024 reduced 30 basis points to 10.1%. Free cash flow estimates improved modestly by 0.2%, but since this includes a 2% improvement in the estimates for 2025 and 2026 and a backloaded reduction in estimates of 3.6%, the CAGR dropped from 7.6% to 6.4%.

The image above shows part of Lockheed Martin’s problem. The company is expected to see margins improve to around 14% in 2026 and 2027. At the same time, cash flow conversion is expected to be lower. Previously, share repurchases were a big driver for upside for the stock and that is no longer the case. Even if we would use the space in the net debt leverage to be used to purchase additional shares, it wouldn’t drive much upside in the stock price. Under the current assumption, 100% of the free cash flow will already be returned to shareholders. The problem for Lockheed Martin is that the company’s appeal for a long time has been that a lot of value could be pushed into the stock price through share repurchases. However, at current price levels that is no longer the case and Lockheed Martin has now strong free cash flow growth. EBITDA is set to improve substantially. The same, however cannot be said about free cash flow, which as I mentioned is expected to be 6.4% annually and basically flat when we take 2025 as a reference year. So, the company is lacking the free cash flow growth to drive meaningful changes in the share price.

Potentially, there is some upside if the 2026 and 2027 cash conversion comes in stronger than currently modelled, but what is clear is that Lockheed Martin lacks a strong growth driver. That is also why I pointed out that Lockheed Martin needs the Golden Dome project. The company has a 23 years dividend growth streak, but we note that the yield is not really attractive and it is also not the case that a strong yield on cost can be built. So, the way the company is performing now and with the current growth prospects, we are still rating Lockheed Martin stock a Buy, but it is inching closer and closer to a hold rating.

Conclusion: Lockheed Martin Appeal Is Fading

Looking at Lockheed Martin, we note that the company is in dire need of a new big defense platform win as free cash flow is expected to be flat in the years ahead and share repurchases do no longer meaningfully drive upside. In terms of upside, we are seeing that Lockheed Martin has roughly the same upside as Northrop Grumman. I have been bullish on the aerospace and defense industry, but we have detected more value in the supply chain, European defense peers, other emerging defense players and mixed end-market contractors than with the US prime defense contractors.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Is This A 'Sleeper Sector' For 2026?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

I’m always on the lookout for “Sleeper Sectors” - groups of stocks that may not be getting talked about much, but that might have a surprise catalyst brewing. Today, let’s look at homebuilders!

This is my first MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which shows the average rate on a 30-year fixed mortgage going back three years. As you can see, we’re getting close to breaking the 6% threshold to the downside. We’re also right around a three-year low.

Chart #1: 30-Year Mortgage Rates

(Source: Freddie Mac)

Now here’s my second MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which shows a much longer-term chart of existing home sales. You can see the huge dip during the pandemic, the big rebound afterward, the sickening slump that followed... and the long period of sideways chop since 2023.

Chart #2: Existing Home Sales

(Source: Trading Economics)

If you look closely at the last few months, though, you can also see that we’ve seen a persistent pickup in sales. Falling financing costs, solid-if-not-spectacular wage growth, and price stagnation in many parts of the country are helping restore a semblance of affordability to homes again. And buyers are starting to notice.

As for homebuilding stocks? Well, the State Street SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB) is up in the past year... but only 7.3%. The iShares US Home Construction ETF (ITB) is up just 4.2%. Both are badly lagging the 19.9% rise in the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) during the same 12-month period.

XHB also owns building supply companies, home furnishing retailers, home improvement stores, and other derivative stocks. So, it’s not technically a “pure-play” home builder ETF. But ITB is much more heavily weighted toward builders like D.R. Horton Inc. (DHI) and Lennar Corp. (LEN)... and even it hasn’t moved much.

So, I think the Sleeper Sector name applies here. And I think you may want to investigate these stocks and funds further for 2026.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Stock Is Running A Bit Hot
data.description:
It seems that I may have turned neutral on The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) too early. Since I downgraded the stock in mid-November, it has advanced by another ~22%. In that analysis, I viewed the fundamentals as stellar, but due to valuation concerns, I decided that some caution was warranted. Goldman Sachs reported their latest earnings just today (Jan 15th), and so we'll analyze the results and valuation to see whether changes to my stance are necessary now.

Below, it is shown that Goldman Sachs is getting back to its roots, and that is helping with their performance. Their flagship Global Banking & Markets segment is leading the way, and strong inflows show that their push into Asset & Wealth Management is strong. While prospects for the business are high right now, I would say that the valuation may be running a bit too hot. The P/B ratio is miles above its historic median and trades at a considerable premium to the financial sector. Therefore, while I'm highly bullish on Goldman Sachs the company, I have decided to stick with my hold rating for the stock.

Their Strategy at a Glance

Before getting to the financials, I believe it is worth mentioning that the firm's shift back to its roots has been a great strategic decision. Goldman Sachs has been a premier investment bank for decades, and so in hindsight, the push into consumer areas with their Marcus platform and the Apple Card (AAPL) were missteps. As you can see above, they have narrowed their strategic focus and are back to doing what they do best. This relatively quick pivot in strategy seems to be paying off.

In addition, the company is very focused on growing their Asset & Wealth Management business, seemingly following in the footsteps of their arch-rival Morgan Stanley (MS). As stated above, this can lead to more durable revenues over time, and that could help to remove some of the cyclicality in their business, which in turn could boost their valuation. Overall, this brief discussion is meant to show that Goldman Sachs has been wise to get back to their roots.

Q4 Results Overview

Let's take a broad view of their earnings before looking closer at their segment results a little later. For 2025 Q4, Goldman Sachs generated total revenues of $13.454 billion. That represents a 3% decline YoY, and they missed consensus expectations of $13.85 billion. However, investors need not be concerned, as they stated this was a result of a $2.26 billion Apple Card markdown in their Platform Solutions segment. Therefore, moving forward, no such drag on performance should be present. As shown on the right, Global Banking & Markets saw strong growth, but their Asset & Wealth Management results may have been a bit weaker than desired as there was a YoY drop in revenues.

In terms of business efficiency, things perhaps could have been a little better. Operating expenses surged by 18% YoY as they reported higher compensation and benefits as well as higher transaction expenses. In terms of profitability, you can see that EPS growth was strong at 17% and that ROE and ROTE expanded both YoY and QoQ. However, note that while the Apple Card transition negatively affected revenues, the net benefit seen in provision for credit losses was a result of a $2.48 billion reserve reduction related to Apple Card. This benefit more than offsets the markdown effects on revenue, and so bottom line figures and profitability ratios are a good reflection of where the firm is really at right now. While Goldman Sachs' top line missed, they beat EPS expectations by $2.25, and so Q4 was overall a solid quarter in my view.

Global Banking & Markets

Goldman Sachs' flagship segment did the heavy lifting in Q4 with net revenues growing by 22%. Investment banking fees were very robust, and that is a pleasant surprise given that my recent analysis on JPMorgan (JPM) showed that activity was a bit sluggish. That said, you can see that equity underwriting saw just 4% growth, and that may be a reflection of how the U.S. Government shutdown affected IPOs. In terms of sales and trading, the firm beat expectations for both FICC and equities. I believe the strength in equities was a key highlight of their Q4 and is likely a reflection of an increase in volatility, particularly in tech stocks, back in November.

It is worth mentioning that M&A advisory will be a core growth driver moving forward. As you can see above, Goldman Sachs has been on top in this space, and recently it was reported that in 2025, the firm advised on 38 of 68 $10 billion+ deals. This shows that they are a clear leader. Due to the forward catalysts listed above, their backlog is now at its highest level in four years, and that suggests to me that M&A will be a key area of focus moving forward.

Asset & Wealth Management

While the headline revenue drop of 1% YoY may be disappointing, a more in-depth look at their AUS shows that this business continues to strengthen. As shown on the right, total net inflows were considerably more than in the previous quarter and the prior year period, as there were strong long-term AUS inflows. In addition, they also saw a decent amount of net market appreciation, which is an indication that their investment performance is solid.

One of the greatest areas of potential within this segment would be alternatives. At the end of their Q4, alternative investments had $420 billion in AUS, and it is shown in the above infographic that they are targeting $750 billion in AUS by 2030. This shows that they are significantly upping their game in this space, and that should be a driver of fee growth moving forward. Alternatives have been, overall, a lucrative area of investments in the financial sector in general, and so Goldman Sachs' push into this area is a promising move in my view.

Valuation Running Hot

Goldman Sachs' fundamentals are currently solid, and their strategy is back to where it should be. The company has some good growth drivers in each of their key segments, and so the outlook does seem to be strong for the firm. That said, the valuation may be running a bit too hot. As you can see in the chart above, their P/B ratio is currently at 2.663, and that is miles above the 5-year median of just 1.305. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha shows that their P/B is at a 106% premium to the Financials sector and overall their valuation is rated as a D-. Therefore, as of this writing, the stock isn't cheap. Goldman Sachs is back to what they do best, and they are seemingly focused on expanding their Asset & Wealth Management business, but in my view, the valuation indicates that there is a bit too much optimism and that investors may be overpaying for strong fundamentals.

Conclusion

I'm very bullish on Goldman Sachs, the company. With Apple Card now behind them, the company is clearly focused on the right areas. The flagship segment is doing well, with M&A being a major opportunity, while inflows and alternative AUS targets show that they are aggressively expanding in the wealth management area as well. Fee growth could help to remove some of the cyclicality in their business. While this does justify a premium over the sector and the historic median, I believe the P/B ratio's now highly elevated level shows that opportunity in the stock is quite limited. Therefore, while the prospects are bright for Goldman Sachs, I have decided to reiterate my hold rating.

data.title: Tracking The Rise Of Islamic Finance
data.description:
In January 2026, the FTSE Yasaar Global Equity Shariah Index Series celebrates its 20th anniversary.

Over the two decades of its existence, this equity benchmark family—established in 2006 via a partnership between FTSE Russell and specialist Shariah consultants Yasaar Limited—has established itself as a key part of the fast-growing Islamic Finance industry. Today, the number of indices within the FTSE Yasaar Shariah Index family exceeds 150 and continues to grow.

Growth of Islamic Finance

In a recent report, the London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG) forecasts that Islamic finance assets worldwide will approach the $10trn mark by the end of this decade, a rapid rate of growth for a relatively new industry: Islamic finance assets only surpassed $1trn in 2010 and reached their second trillion in 2014.

The Islamic finance industry has now expanded its footprint to 140 countries, says LSEG, and its asset growth is not just a reflection of the growing savings of the world’s Muslim population.

While Muslim-majority countries in the Middle East and Southeast Asia continue to dominate the industry, developments and growth in other markets continue. This is in large part because of the intrinsically ethical nature of Shariah finance (Islamic equity indices screen out companies involved in conventional finance, alcohol and intoxicants, gambling, weapons, arms and defence manufacturing).

In particular, the United Kingdom, LSEG’s and FTSE Russell’s home market, is now a key hub for listings of green and sustainable sukuk (Islamic debt instruments).

Figure 1: Islamic finance assets growth

The FTSE Russell/Yasaar partnership

The FTSE Yasaar Global Equity Shariah Index Series is based on FTSE Russell’s flagship global equity benchmark, the FTSE Global Equity Index Series (FTSE GEIS). The starting set of companies included in the FTSE GEIS is then screened for Shariah compliance (compliant with the precepts of Islamic finance) by Yasaar.

Majid Dawood, Yasaar’s founder and chief executive, explains the distinctive features of an Islamic approach to equity investment.

“Islamic finance is about financing real growth with real money,” says Mr. Dawood.

“Money shouldn’t make money: building things should make money and selling things should make money.”

“One of the important ideas in Islam is that you cannot sell fruit from a tree before it has appeared. That’s because you don’t know what the result will be and whether the tree will produce fruit at all.”

“So Islamic finance is about selling what you have, not what you might have in future.”

These principles are embedded in the FTSE Yasaar Global Equity Shariah Index Series rules in two ways: business activity screening removes companies involved in undesired activities, and financial screening removes companies with excessive reliance on debt, interest or leverage.

Importantly, Yasaar has always had a conservative approach to screening: for example, it ensures Shariah compliance by rating debt ratio limits as a percentage of total assets (rather than as a percentage of trailing market capitalisation, which is a more volatile measure).

Yasaar’s screening process is also overseen and monitored by the company’s Shariah board, which reviews and audits the screening process at regular intervals. Shariah-compliant constituents are updated daily and audit trails or any changes to a company’s Shariah status are available to the Board for review.

Performance in line with the global equity market

The primary use cases of the FTSE Global Equity Shariah Index Series are as a reference benchmark for the performance of Shariah-compliant equity portfolios and as an underlying performance target for investment products that meet the requirements of Islamic investors.

If the investment performance of the indices within the series is secondary to these requirements, in practice the net financial impact on a global equity investor of preferring a Shariah approach has been marginal.

The total return of the FTSE Shariah All-World index over the two decades since its launch has been very much in line with that of the FTSE All-World index (the most widely used investable benchmark from the FTSE Global Equity Index Series).

Figure 2: Two decades of the FTSE Shariah All-World Index

Poised for further growth

As the FTSE Yasaar Global Equity Shariah Index Series embarks on its third decade, what lies ahead?

The prospects for the Islamic finance sector seem bright: Islamic finance services are now available in a range of markets around the world, the sector can now compete directly with its conventional counterparts, and there is healthy product innovation in the form of digital sukuk, Islamic fintech and further integration with other sustainable finance approaches.

The demand for indices and benchmarks that embed Islamic investment principles could therefore grow substantially further in the coming years.

data.title: Turn Oklo's Hype Into Income
data.description:
Over the last year or so, I’ve covered nuclear startup Oklo (OKLO) four times, at different points highlighting the company’s various strengths and weaknesses.

In December of 2024, I called shares a Strong Buy on the back of the company’s significant nuclear ambitions, attractive entry price point, and long-term potential.

With no serious technical hurdles to overcome, I viewed the company’s innovative engagement with the NRC’s regulatory process as bringing dynamism to a once-stagnant nuclear market. With an attractive business model to boot, I thought shares appeared underpriced.

Throughout 2025, the market validated my thesis, as shares eventually skyrocketed roughly 800% from my initial bullish call:

More recently, in late summer of 2025, I downgraded shares due to significant valuation concerns.

Sure, nuclear power might be the future, and Oklo might be the fastest, most well-capitalized mover in the market. But with significant execution hurdles, a long timeline for buildout, and economics that have yet to be proven, I viewed shares as too risky.

Fast forward to the present, and the company’s stock has remained volatile, more than doubling (and then halving) throughout the fall and winter season.

While management has done a good job executing, continuing to build momentum with new clients and plans for new facilities, shares are still priced at an exorbitant premium.

Thus, at the present moment, I view selling put options on the stock as the most attractive way to generate returns here.

Sure, you could buy and hold shares over the long term - and I expect that eventually you’d be rewarded - but for the time being, selling put options, generating a substantial cash-on-cash return, and retaining the chance to buy shares in the event of a significant dip looks like the best way to play it.

Today, I’ll lay out recent updates with OKLO, highlight the valuation, and explain why I believe selling out-of-the-money put options is an unbelievable risk-return trade right now.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials & Price

When it comes to Oklo, the company has no real financials to speak of, as the business does not yet produce any real revenues. As a design / development stage company, the firm has identified an attractive market segment and is building toward future commercial operations, but the business doesn’t yet make any money.

As a result, valuing the stock and analyzing the financials by any traditional means is relatively difficult.

This, I suspect, is responsible for a great deal of the volatility around the stock. As a business's prospects become less clear, the wider the potential range of outcomes, which has driven the wild moves.

Thus, with no real financials or traditional value metrics to go off of, three key questions linger over the business for investors:

  • First, is there significant demand for nuclear power?
  • Secondly, does the company have enough capital to 'get there'?
  • And finally, is the stock priced to allow for additional gains?

Let’s go through each of these a little bit more closely.

If you’ve read my previous articles on Oklo, then you know that I’m particularly bullish on nuclear energy. The U.S. faces a significant power supply gap between now and 2030, as increasing demands from AI, electric vehicles, and population growth overburden the current electrical grid.

At the same time, the world is attempting to go through a clean energy transition away from dirty fossil fuels. When it comes to power load stability, lack of emissions, and efficiency, nuclear power is the clear choice for society.

As old natural gas plants go offline in the next five to ten years - and our energy demands grow - no power source except for nuclear is poised to truly tackle the problem.

Additionally, increasing cyber threats from foreign countries make a large-scale national grid appear more vulnerable than ever. All of these ingredients add up to small nuclear reactors being the way forward. Providing localized, efficient, clean power to data centers, factories, hospitals, and regional interconnections makes small nuclear reactors appear like the most attractive energy generation option going forward.

The company has proven this out with its business model and recent contract wins. Oklo’s proposed business model vertically integrates the nuclear industry, putting construction, generation, and commercialization efforts all under the same roof:

First, our build-own-operate model allows us to sell power directly to customers under long-term contracts. That creates recurring revenue and streamlines the regulatory process by keeping ownership and operational control within Oklo.

Similarly, if the company manages to get its first fuel enrichment plant in Tennessee online, part of the nuclear supply chain will also be owned by Oklo.

At the same time, Meta’s recent power purchase agreement with Oklo indicates that there is significant demand from major energy users if and when the company can scale up its unique 'powerhouse' concept:

In short, at a base level, I believe that there is significant demand for nuclear power, and thus, there will be significant revenue potential for Oklo once it moves past the development stage and into generation.

Secondly, will the company have enough capital to get there?

Building nuclear reactors, even small ones, is a capital-intensive process, and getting past regulatory hurdles takes persistence, grit, and significant admin expense. The good news is that the company’s recent stock price rally has significantly eased the company’s financial burdens

As shares have skyrocketed throughout 2025, the company has announced two significant share sale programs for $1.5 billion and $3.5 billion, respectively. This allows the company to tap capital markets when it needs liquidity.

At the same time, the company’s balance sheet lists more than $1.2 billion in total assets, most of which remain liquid, with no real debt to speak of:

In the company’s early investor materials, management expected 50 MW powerhouses to cost roughly $142 million initially, dropping to $116 million per unit after efficiencies of scale kick in:

Even if costs turn out to be triple management's early expectations, there is still significant liquidity to build a number of functional powerhouses and make significant progress on the fuel enrichment plant planned in Tennessee.

Additionally, there’s no explicit timeline pressure provided by interest payments or debt coming due. As a result, I view significant development delays for Oklo to be less of a material negative than they would be for other development-stage companies. There’s a lot of flexibility here.

In sum, I believe that Oklo’s ongoing execution - including breaking ground on its first powerhouse in Idaho - and significant capital reserves make the company’s nuclear vision possible.

Finally, the third question is whether the stock is priced for additional gains in the event of a successful commercial launch.

This is where my opinion diverges from the market.

If you look at analyst estimates, they’re currently expecting the company to produce negative, but steadily improving, losses through 2029, until the company breaks even in 2030:

This is because Oklo does not plan to start bringing in revenues until 2027 or 2028, as the first powerhouse plant is proven out, regulatory decisions come through, and a more meaningful buildout and fulfillment of its PPA contracts can commence in earnest.

For my part, I mostly agree with this timeline. I don’t expect that the company will produce positive earnings until 2030. However, if you look at the unit economics per power plant, the stock appears unbelievably expensive at present.

If you assume a per-powerhouse annual revenue of roughly $36 million, ongoing expenses of $10 million per year, and the 4-5 year payback period, the return on capital is obviously attractive:

However, depending on how many powerhouses the company can produce by 2030, there’s significant variance in my gross profit expectations. If the company can achieve ten powerhouses by 2030, then you’re looking at roughly $350 million in annual revenue, which could produce an annual gross profit of $280 million.

This implies a 2030 gross profit multiple of roughly 54x. In other words, if the company only achieves ten powerhouses by 2030, then the stock appears incredibly, incredibly expensive. That's more expensive than Palantir (PLTR), for reference.

If the company can produce twenty powerhouses, then these numbers double, and the valuation halves to roughly 27 times gross profit. For a result that could be five-plus years away, this is still incredibly expensive.

As a result, even if the company can reach a successful commercial launch and produce dozens of powerhouses over the next five years (which I believe is ambitious), the stock already appears somewhat priced for that. Remember, at the end of the day, this is a utility business, and one that will eventually have significant regulation on, or volatility in, its rates.

As a result, I think shares look like a hold for now, even with the potential exponentiality of far, far off EPS estimates.

The Trade

So - owning Oklo could be a great long-term bet, but shares are too expensively priced to realize much upside in the short and medium term. With a setup like this, I view selling puts as the best risk-reward option for your money.

When you sell a put option, you’re telling the market that you’re willing to buy shares of a company at a certain strike price between now and the expiration of the option.

In return for committing to this, the buyer pays you a cash premium.

Essentially, you’re selling insurance that if the stock goes down, you’ll buy it off of someone at a guaranteed price.

Given that I’d love to own Oklo, but the high valuation presents an issue, selling puts is a beautiful way to capitalize on that view.

For Oklo, selling the May 15 $50 strike put options looks like the best available choice:

With 120 days between now and expiration, this trade keeps you involved with Oklo for roughly four months, and in my experience, I've found that 70 - 140 days is the 'sweet spot' for selling OTM options. This is because you get some flexibility (you're not locked in for years), and you can sell far out of the money while still generating a solid return. For shorter DTE contracts, the nominal yields are quite low.

With this trade, if the stock dips beneath $50, - which is roughly half the current price - then you’d be assigned shares of the company. As I described earlier, I believe that a much cheaper price offers a much better entry point, so this isn't necessarily a negative.

On the reward side, these options currently pay a credit of $245 per contract. Given that these options only require $4,755 in margin to hold in your brokerage account, selling these puts produces a cash-on-cash return of 5.15%, which annualizes to nearly ~16%.

This is a much better yield than you’ll get from bonds, high-dividend stocks, business development companies, MLPs, or nearly any other type of income-producing asset.

At the same time, you preserve your chance to buy Oklo at a significant discount.

If Oklo trades up, sideways, or down (but still above $50) over the next four months, then you get to keep the cash premium free and clear.

There are some risks to be aware of. On one hand, if OKLO's stock performs well, then selling puts doesn't capture as much of that upside, so there's potential opportunity cost here. Additionally, if OKLO's stock price craters over the next 4 months, then you could be stuck with OKLO over the long term, with significant capital impairment. This isn't a different risk profile than simply holding the stock, but it's worth being aware of.

Summary

Overall, though, I believe that selling put options in Oklo is the best risk-reward move investors can make with the stock right now.

The company’s prospects appear bright, but the stock price reflects that.

As a result, I don’t view shares as a compelling buy.

That said, selling put options with a 50% break-even and a 16% annualized yield over the next 120 days looks like a great win-win trade. Either you get your cash back free and clear, or you are assigned a great company at a better price than it’s currently trading.

Cheers~
